tutors.name: Alex Chen
tutors.description: Alex specializes in data structures, algorithms, and system-level technical interviews. Expects clean code, big-O analysis, and thorough edge case coverage. Won't let you skip the hard parts.

tutors.name: Priya Sharma
tutors.description: Priya coaches behavioral interviews using the STAR method. She helps candidates present authentic, structured stories that resonate with hiring managers across all levels.

tutors.name: Morgan Blake
tutors.description: Morgan covers system design at scale, product sense, and cross-functional leadership. Brings FAANG-level expectations to every session and pushes for depth in trade-off reasoning.

packages.name: Behavioral Interview
packages.description: Master STAR storytelling for leadership, conflict, teamwork, and impact questions used by top companies.

packages.name: Technical Screen
packages.description: Full-stack technical screen covering programming, databases, REST APIs, and architecture basics.

packages.name: DSA Deep Dive
packages.description: LeetCode-style coding interview with real-time problem solving, complexity analysis, and edge cases.

packages.name: HR Round
packages.description: Salary negotiation, culture fit, expectations alignment, and career goals discussion.

packages.name: Product Manager
packages.description: Product sense, metrics definition, roadmap prioritization, and stakeholder management scenarios.

packages.name: System Design
packages.description: Design scalable distributed systems — URL shorteners, feeds, messaging platforms, and payment systems.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Welcome! Let's start with a classic opener — tell me about a time you led a team through a challenging situation.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Sure! At my previous role, um, we had a critical production outage. I basically had to coordinate like four different teams simultaneously.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Good scenario. Walk me through the specific steps you took to coordinate those teams.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: So first I set up a war room — virtual since we were remote — and assigned clear ownership to each team. I made sure everyone had, um, a single source of truth for the incident status.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Excellent use of structure. What was the measurable outcome of your coordination efforts?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: We reduced downtime from an estimated 4 hours to 47 minutes. Customer impact was minimal and we shipped a post-mortem within 48 hours with 12 action items.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Strong quantified result. What would you do differently in hindsight?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I'd set up better runbooks ahead of time so the coordination wouldn't require as much real-time decision making. Basically reactive preparation rather than being reactive in the moment.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Given an unsorted array of integers, find two numbers that add up to a target sum. Return their indices. What's your approach?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Classic two-sum. I'd use a hash map for O(n) time. For each element, check if target minus current exists in the map, otherwise store it.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Good. Now what if the array is sorted — can you do better in terms of space complexity?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Yes — with sorted input I can use two pointers. O(n) time, O(1) space. Left pointer at start, right at end, move based on sum comparison.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Correct. Now extend this to three-sum. Constraints: find all unique triplets summing to zero.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Sort first, then for each element use two-pointer on the rest. Total O(n²) time. I need to handle duplicates by skipping equal adjacent elements.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Good on duplicate handling — most candidates miss that. What about the maximum subarray problem?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Kadane's algorithm. Track current sum and max seen. If current sum drops negative, reset to current element. O(n) time, O(1) space.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Design a URL shortening service like bit.ly. Start with requirements clarification.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Functional: create short URLs, redirect to original, track analytics. Non-functional: 100M URLs/day, sub-100ms redirect latency, 99.99% availability.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Good scope. How would you generate the short codes uniquely at scale across multiple servers?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Base62 encoding of an auto-increment ID, or use a distributed ID generator like Snowflake for true horizontal scaling without coordination.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: What about caching? Where would you apply it and what eviction policy makes sense here?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: CDN for the redirect layer globally, Redis in-memory cache for hot URLs close to the app servers. LRU eviction since access patterns are highly skewed — 20% of URLs get 80% of traffic.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: What are your salary expectations for this role?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Based on my research and experience level, I'm targeting between $145,000 and $165,000 base, with flexibility depending on the total compensation package including equity.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: What's your current notice period?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I have a 30-day notice period, but I've successfully negotiated it to two weeks in the past when timing was critical for the new employer.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Explain the difference between REST and GraphQL. When would you choose one over the other?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: REST uses fixed endpoints and HTTP methods — predictable, cacheable. GraphQL gives clients a single endpoint where they specify exactly what data they need, avoiding over-fetching.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Good. What are the real-world trade-offs of GraphQL in production at scale?

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: N+1 query problem if not handled with DataLoader, complexity in caching since queries are dynamic, potentially more tooling overhead and schema management at scale.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Tell me about a time you had to make a difficult decision without all the information you needed.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: We had to decide whether to delay a product launch by two weeks. Data was incomplete — early signals suggested stability issues. We made the call to launch on schedule with enhanced monitoring.

transcript.speaker: ai
transcript.text: Implement a binary search tree with insert, search, and in-order traversal.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: I'll define a Node class with left/right pointers and a value. Insert recursively comparing values. In-order: left subtree, root, right subtree — gives sorted output.

strengths: Excellent use of quantified outcomes — reducing downtime from 4h to 47min is highly compelling to interviewers
Clear STAR structure maintained throughout every response without prompting
Demonstrated cross-functional ownership and leadership instincts consistently
improvements: Reduce filler word usage (um, basically, like) — appeared 14 times in 32 minutes, impacting perceived confidence
Expand the Reflection portion of STAR — what did you personally learn from each experience?
Broaden your story bank — you used the outage story twice, build 5-6 distinct scenarios

answerFeedback.question: Tell me about a time you led a team through a challenging situation.
answerFeedback.userAnswer: Coordinated 4 teams during a production outage, set up virtual war room, assigned clear ownership. Reduced downtime from 4h to 47min, shipped post-mortem within 48h.
answerFeedback.feedback: Strong answer with excellent quantified impact. Missing: learning and reflection component. Add one sentence on what you'd do differently to complete the story arc.

answerFeedback.question: Describe a conflict you had with a colleague and how you resolved it.
answerFeedback.userAnswer: Had a disagreement with a senior engineer on API design philosophy. Proposed a time-boxed spike to test both approaches with real data. Data guided the final decision.
answerFeedback.feedback: Good conflict resolution using evidence over opinion. The answer feels brief — add emotional context and acknowledge the relationship aftermath to humanize it.

answerFeedback.question: What's your biggest professional failure?
answerFeedback.userAnswer: Underestimated a database migration complexity and missed a deadline by 3 weeks. Worked overtime, communicated proactively with stakeholders, and added buffer to all future estimates.
answerFeedback.feedback: Honest and shows real accountability. Strengthen the systematic change section — what process did you implement so this specific failure mode can't repeat?

anal_001.report:
## Session Analysis Report

**Overall Performance:** Strong — Above Average

You demonstrated genuine leadership capability and a results-oriented mindset throughout this session. Your quantified outcomes set you apart from most candidates at this level.

### What Worked Well

- **Structured thinking:** Every answer had a clear setup, execution, and measurable result
- **Specificity:** Concrete numbers (47 minutes, 4 teams, 48 hours) make your stories believable and memorable
- **Ownership language:** Phrases like "I set up" and "I assigned" show agency without undermining team credit
- **Proactive follow-through:** Mentioning the post-mortem shows systemic thinking

### Areas to Strengthen

- **Filler words:** 14 instances of 'um', 'basically', and 'like' in 32 minutes. This is your highest-impact improvement area. Practice recording yourself and replaying.
- **Reflection depth:** Interviewers at senior levels specifically want to know what you *learned*, not just what happened and what resulted
- **Story breadth:** You leaned on the production outage story in two separate answers. Build a story matrix with at least 5-6 distinct situations mapped to common behavioral themes

### Recommended Next Steps

1. Record yourself answering "Tell me about a failure" — focus exclusively on the Reflection section and practice until it sounds natural
2. Review the STAR-AR extension (Situation, Task, Action, Result, *Reflection*) — the added R is what differentiates senior candidates
3. Build a story matrix: map 6 experiences to 12 common behavioral themes
4. Practice 2 more behavioral sessions this week to cement the improvements

topReplacements.exampleInContext: Instead of 'I had to, um, coordinate...' simply pause and say 'I had to coordinate...'
Instead of 'I basically set up a war room' say 'I established a dedicated incident war room'
Instead of 'like four different teams' say 'four cross-functional teams'

sentenceRewrites.original: I basically had to coordinate like four different teams simultaneously.
sentenceRewrites.rewritten: I coordinated four cross-functional teams simultaneously.
sentenceRewrites.improvement: Removed two filler words, more precise and confident phrasing

sentenceRewrites.original: I made sure everyone had, um, a single source of truth.
sentenceRewrites.rewritten: I established a shared incident dashboard as the single source of truth.
sentenceRewrites.improvement: Removed filler, added specificity that shows structured thinking

sentenceRewrites.original: Basically reactive preparation rather than being reactive in the moment.
sentenceRewrites.rewritten: Proactive runbook preparation rather than reactive improvisation during incidents.
sentenceRewrites.improvement: Eliminated filler, parallel structure, professional vocabulary

strengthsInCommunication: Consistent use of active voice throughout all answers
Clear subject-verb-object sentence structure makes answers easy to follow
Quantified claims add credibility and specificity

strengths: Immediate and correct pattern recognition on two-sum and three-sum variants
Accurately identified space-time trade-offs across different input constraints
Articulated complexity analysis unprompted — shows strong algorithmic foundation
improvements: Verbalize your reasoning process more — you jump to solutions without showing the path
Ask clarifying questions before coding (duplicates allowed? negative numbers? sorted input?)
Test with edge cases — you tested the happy path only, missing empty array and no-solution cases

answerFeedback.question: Find two numbers that add up to a target sum.
answerFeedback.userAnswer: Hash map approach O(n) time, two-pointer for sorted input O(n) time O(1) space.
answerFeedback.feedback: Correct and efficient. Missing: clarifying questions about constraints. Edge cases to add: empty array, no valid pair, duplicate elements.

answerFeedback.question: Extend to three-sum (unique triplets summing to zero).
answerFeedback.userAnswer: Sort + two-pointer, O(n²) time, O(1) extra space, skip duplicates on outer and inner loops.
answerFeedback.feedback: Correct complexity and correctly mentioned duplicate handling. Strong answer — consider also mentioning the hash map O(n²) space approach as an alternative.

answerFeedback.question: Find the maximum subarray sum.
answerFeedback.userAnswer: Kadane's algorithm. Track current sum and global max. Reset current to element if it goes negative. O(n) time, O(1) space.
answerFeedback.feedback: Excellent. Clean explanation of Kadane's. Consider mentioning the divide-and-conquer O(n log n) approach to show depth.

anal_002.report:
## DSA Session Analysis

**Overall Performance:** Competent — Approaching Strong

Solid algorithmic fundamentals with room to grow in communication and edge case coverage. Your pattern recognition is a real strength.

### Technical Strengths

- **Pattern recognition:** Identified optimal approaches (hash map, two-pointer, Kadane's) immediately and correctly
- **Complexity analysis:** Consistently analyzed time and space without being prompted — this is a differentiator
- **Trade-off awareness:** Correctly identified when to prefer space efficiency over time savings

### Growth Areas

- **Clarifying questions:** Always clarify constraints before writing a single line. Interviewers explicitly reward this — it shows real-world engineering instinct.
- **Think out loud:** Your final solutions are correct, but the path there is invisible. Narrate your decision-making in real time.
- **Edge cases:** Build a mental checklist: empty input, single element, all negatives, duplicates, no valid answer.

### Recommended Practice Plan

1. LeetCode focus: Two Sum (1), 3Sum (15), Maximum Subarray (53) — review editorial solutions
2. Practice the 'rubber duck' technique: explain your solution aloud before coding it
3. Spend 10 minutes on edge cases for every problem you solve

strengths: Methodical requirements gathering — established scale and constraints before jumping to architecture
Correct identification of distributed ID generation need (Snowflake/base62)
LRU cache reasoning was sound, justified by realistic access pattern analysis
improvements: Rate limiting was never addressed — this is critical for any public API (abuse prevention, cost control)
Database sharding strategy was vague — be specific about shard key choice and hot-shard mitigation
No mention of observability — monitoring, alerting, and distributed tracing are table stakes at senior level

answerFeedback.question: Design a URL shortening service at 100M URLs/day.
answerFeedback.userAnswer: Requirements clarification, base62 encoding, Redis LRU cache, CDN for redirect layer.
answerFeedback.feedback: Solid foundation with good justification for each choice. Critical gaps: rate limiting, abuse prevention, analytics pipeline, multi-region failover strategy.

answerFeedback.question: How would you handle database scaling at this volume?
answerFeedback.userAnswer: Horizontal sharding with consistent hashing, read replicas for analytics queries.
answerFeedback.feedback: Correct direction. Be more specific — shard by hash(short_code) to distribute evenly, explain how you'd handle hot shards for viral URLs.

anal_003.report:
## System Design Analysis

**Overall Performance:** Developing — Needs Depth

You show a solid grasp of distributed systems basics, but senior-level system design interviews at FAANG expect depth in observability, failure modes, and abuse prevention.

### What You Did Well

- Requirements gathering was methodical and covered functional and non-functional requirements
- Caching layer reasoning was well-justified with real access pattern analysis
- ID generation showed awareness of distributed constraints

### Critical Gaps to Address

- **Rate limiting:** Every public API needs it. Discuss token bucket (burst-friendly) or sliding window (strict). Apply at edge, per-IP, and per-API-key levels.
- **Observability:** Mention Prometheus metrics (request rate, error rate, latency p99), distributed tracing (Jaeger/OpenTelemetry), and alert thresholds.
- **Failure modes:** What happens when the cache is cold? When the DB primary fails? When a region goes down? Show you've thought about the unhappy path.

### 30-Day Study Plan

1. Read: *Designing Data-Intensive Applications* (Kleppmann) — Chapters 5-6 on replication and partitioning
2. Practice: Design Pastebin, TinyURL, then Instagram Feed in order of increasing complexity
3. Add a mandatory "failure modes" and "observability" section to every design you practice

stats.practiceTip: Before answering any behavioral question, take 3 seconds to think. Interviewers expect and respect the pause — it signals structured thinking, not uncertainty.

transcript_demo.speaker: ai
transcript_demo.text: Welcome to your mock interview. I'm ready when you are. Let's start — tell me about yourself and why you're excited about this opportunity.

transcript_demo.speaker: user
transcript_demo.text: I'm a software engineer with 5 years of experience in backend systems and distributed architectures. I've led teams at growth-stage startups and worked on systems serving millions of daily active users.

transcript_demo.speaker: ai
transcript_demo.text: Solid background. What specifically draws you to this type of role over others you might be exploring right now?

transcript_demo.speaker: user
transcript_demo.text: The technical depth here is compelling. The scale problems your team is solving — particularly in real-time data processing — align well with where I've focused the last two years of my career.

transcript_demo.speaker: ai
transcript_demo.text: Good. Let's dig into that real-time work. Walk me through the most complex system you've designed or significantly contributed to.

transcript_demo.speaker: user
transcript_demo.text: I designed an event-driven notification system processing 40 million events per day across 12 microservices. Used Kafka for message queuing, Redis for deduplication state, and built a custom retry mechanism with exponential backoff.

transcript_demo.speaker: ai
transcript_demo.text: What was the hardest technical challenge you faced in that system, and how did you resolve it?

messages.text:
Hey! I've reviewed your latest behavioral session with Priya. You scored **86/100** — that's above average for this package. Here's the quick summary:

**Wins:**
- Your production outage story had excellent quantified impact (47 min vs 4h)
- Strong STAR structure maintained throughout
- Consistent ownership language

**Top 2 improvements:**
- Filler words appeared 14 times — impacting perceived confidence
- Add reflection to your stories — what did *you* personally learn?

Want me to drill any specific area?
What should I focus on to improve my score the most?

Based on your last 3 sessions, here are your **top 3 focus areas** ranked by impact-per-effort:

1. **Filler word reduction** — 14 instances in 32 min hurts perceived confidence significantly. Target: under 5 per session. Highest ROI improvement.
2. **Reflection depth** — Senior interviewers explicitly probe for what you *learned*. Adding 2 sentences to each answer could add 5-8 points.
3. **Story breadth** — You've used the outage story in 2 of 3 recent sessions. Build a bank of 6+ distinct scenarios mapped to themes.

Which do you want to tackle first?

Your DSA session with Alex scored **79/100**. Your fundamentals are solid — pattern recognition is genuinely strong. The main gap is **communicating your reasoning** in real time.

Here's your **7-day DSA sprint plan:**

**Days 1–2:** Arrays & Hash Maps (Two Sum variants, Group Anagrams, Sliding Window)
**Days 3–4:** Trees & Graphs (BFS/DFS, Lowest Common Ancestor, Cycle Detection)
**Days 5–6:** Dynamic Programming (Coin Change, LCS, House Robber)
**Day 7:** Full mock session + targeted review

Ready to start Day 1?
Can you explain the two-pointer technique?

**Two-pointer technique** — uses two indices moving through a data structure to reduce O(n²) brute force down to O(n).

**When to reach for it:**
- Sorted array problems
- Finding pairs/triplets with a target sum
- Palindrome / string matching
- Removing duplicates in-place

**Classic pattern — Two Sum (sorted input):**
```
left = 0, right = n - 1
while left < right:
  total = arr[left] + arr[right]
  if total == target:  return [left, right]
  elif total < target: left++
  else:                right--
```
Time: **O(n)**, Space: **O(1)**

**Key interview tip:** Always state your constraints assumption ("assuming sorted input") before applying this pattern — it signals that you understand when it applies.

actions.label: Practice Two Sum
actions.trigger: Give me a two sum problem to practice right now

messages.text:
I reviewed your system design session with Morgan. Score: **72/100**. You have a real foundation here — the fundamentals are right. But Morgan flagged **3 critical gaps** that FAANG interviewers always probe:

1. **Rate limiting** — zero mention of abuse prevention, which is critical for any public-facing API
2. **Observability** — no metrics, distributed tracing, or alerting strategy
3. **Failure modes** — no discussion of component failure handling or graceful degradation

Addressing just these three could move you from 72 → 88+ on your next attempt.

files.name: STAR Method Guide.md
files.content:
# STAR Method Guide

## What is STAR?

STAR is a structured technique for answering behavioral interview questions clearly and compellingly.

- **S**ituation — Set the scene and provide necessary context
- **T**ask — Describe your specific responsibility in that situation
- **A**ction — Explain the specific steps *you* took (use "I", not "we")
- **R**esult — Share the outcomes, preferably quantified

## Extended: STAR-AR

Add a fifth element for senior roles:
- **R**eflection — What did you personally learn? What would you do differently?

## Time Allocation

- Situation: 10-15% of answer time
- Task: 10% of answer time
- **Action: 50-60% of answer time** (this is what interviewers evaluate)
- Result: 20% of answer time
- Reflection: 5-10% (senior roles)

## Quantification Tips

- Convert vague results: "improved performance" → "reduced p99 latency from 800ms to 120ms"
- Include scale: "served 2M+ daily users", "managed $4M budget"
- Use percentages AND absolutes: "20% reduction = 4 engineer-weeks saved per quarter"

## Story Matrix Template

| Situation | Leadership | Conflict | Failure | Innovation | Collaboration |
|-----------|-----------|----------|---------|------------|---------------|
| Story 1   | ✓         |          | ✓       |            |               |
| Story 2   |           | ✓        |         | ✓          |               |
| Story 3   | ✓         |          |         |            | ✓             |

## Common Mistakes

- Too much time on Situation, not enough on Action
- Vague results ("it went well") instead of specific outcomes
- Using "we" when you should take personal ownership with "I"
- Skipping the Reflection — critical for senior candidates
- Not practicing out loud — writing it is not enough

files.name: DSA Patterns Cheatsheet.md
files.content:
# DSA Patterns Cheatsheet

## Two Pointers

**Use when:** sorted arrays, pairs/triplets, palindromes, in-place operations
**Complexity:** O(n) time, O(1) space
**Problems:** Two Sum (sorted), 3Sum, Container with Most Water, Valid Palindrome

```python
left, right = 0, len(arr) - 1
while left < right:
    # process
    left += 1  # or right -= 1 based on condition
```

## Sliding Window

**Use when:** contiguous subarray/substring with max/min condition
**Complexity:** O(n) time, O(k) space
**Problems:** Max Sum Subarray of Size K, Longest Substring Without Repeat

```python
window_start = 0
for window_end in range(len(arr)):
    # expand window
    while condition_violated:
        # shrink window
        window_start += 1
```

## BFS / DFS

**BFS:** shortest path, level-order traversal, minimum steps
**DFS:** explore all paths, backtracking, connected components
**Complexity:** O(V + E) for graphs

## Dynamic Programming

**Recognize when:** overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure
**Approach:** top-down (memoization) or bottom-up (tabulation)
**Classic problems:** Coin Change, Knapsack, LCS, LIS, Edit Distance

**Template:**
```python
dp = [default_value] * (n + 1)
dp[base_case] = base_value
for i in range(...):
    dp[i] = f(dp[i-1], dp[i-2], ...)
```

## Binary Search

**Use when:** sorted data, search space can be halved each step
**Complexity:** O(log n)

```python
left, right = 0, len(arr) - 1
while left <= right:
    mid = left + (right - left) // 2  # avoid overflow
    if arr[mid] == target: return mid
    elif arr[mid] < target: left = mid + 1
    else: right = mid - 1
```

## Hash Map Patterns

- Frequency counting (anagram detection, majority element)
- Two-sum complement lookup
- Grouping (anagram groups, classification)
- Caching computed results

## Complexity Quick Reference

| Operation | Array | Linked List | Hash Map | BST | Heap |
|-----------|-------|-------------|----------|-----|------|
| Access    | O(1)  | O(n)        | O(1)     | O(log n) | O(1) |
| Search    | O(n)  | O(n)        | O(1)     | O(log n) | O(n) |
| Insert    | O(n)  | O(1)        | O(1)     | O(log n) | O(log n) |
| Delete    | O(n)  | O(1)        | O(1)     | O(log n) | O(log n) |

rootFiles.name: Quick Reference.md
rootFiles.content:
# Quick Reference — Interview Prep

## This Week's Goals

- [ ] Practice 2 behavioral questions (focus on reflection section)
- [ ] Review system design: rate limiting patterns
- [ ] Watch: observability in distributed systems (30 min)
- [ ] DSA: complete sliding window pattern problems

## Interview Day Reminders

- **Pause before answering.** Silence is fine — it signals structured thinking.
- **Ask clarifying questions early** — always before coding or designing.
- **Quantify everything** — numbers beat adjectives every time.
- **Think out loud** — interviewers evaluate process, not just answers.

## Emergency Phrases

- "That's a great question. Let me think through it for a moment..."
- "My initial instinct is X, but let me verify by considering Y first..."
- "I've seen a similar pattern before. Let me apply that approach and see if it holds here..."
- "I want to make sure I understand the constraints before I commit to an approach."

## Key Metrics to Remember

- Notification system: 40M events/day, 12 microservices
- Production outage: reduced from 4h to 47min
- Team size: led 4 cross-functional teams
